metadata.summary: PLG uses the product itself as the primary acquisition engine: self-serve onboarding, "aha moment" under 5 minutes, usage-gated upgrades, and PQL scoring for sales-assist handoff.

content.title: Product-Led Growth (PLG)
content.summary: PLG uses the product itself as the primary acquisition, activation, and expansion engine: self-serve onboarding, a clearly defined "aha moment" under 5 minutes, usage-gated upgrade triggers, and PQL scoring that hands off to sales-assist at the right moment. The agent workflow instruments the funnel, generates activation hypotheses, ships the smallest in-product change behind a feature flag, and gates experimentation behind SRM check and pre-registered MDE.

why.p: Traditional sales-led growth economics break down below ~$25k ACV — the cost per SDR exceeds the contract value. PLG companies achieve higher revenue per employee (Ahrefs reached $40M ARR with 40 people) because the product itself drives the conversion loop. Without instrumentation-first discipline, agents optimise on uninstrumented funnels and hill-climb noise; without a usage-gate in the free tier, there is no upgrade trigger.

list.item: SaaS / dev tools / API products where users can self-onboard without sales (Linear, Vercel, PostHog pattern).
ACV under ~$25k where sales-led economics break down; freemium → paid conversion is the growth lever.
Product produces measurable in-app events (signup, activation step, feature use) that can drive PQL scoring.
Bottom-up motion targeting individual contributors who later expand to teams (Slack, Notion, Figma pattern).
Existing product with traffic but flat activation — room for funnel instrumentation and onboarding redesign.
Highly regulated, procurement-heavy enterprise sales (defense, banking core) — buyer is not the user.
Products requiring data integration, on-prem deploy, or contracts before any value can be shown.
ACV above ~$100k where one closed deal pays for many SDRs; sales-led ROI dominates.
Pre-PMF: PLG amplifies a working loop; on a broken product it industrialises churn.
Network products without single-player value — optimising activation has no payoff if the user lands alone.

rule.statement: Build PLG on four principles: self-serve onboarding (users activate without sales intervention), time-to-value optimization ("aha moment" under 5 minutes), product virality (built-in sharing and collaboration), and expansion revenue (upsell through usage, not sales calls).
rule.rationale: PLG companies achieve higher revenue per employee because the product replaces costly sales motion at the SMB/prosumer tier; without all four principles the loop is incomplete — viral without self-serve requires human handoff, expansion without a usage gate requires sales calls.

list.item: Activation Rate: % users completing key value actions — target above 40%
Time-to-Value (TTV): minutes to "aha moment" — target under 5 minutes
Product Qualified Leads (PQLs): users showing buying signals — track weekly
Expansion Revenue Rate: revenue from self-serve upgrades — target above 30% of total
Net Revenue Retention (NRR): revenue retained plus expansion — target above 120%

rule.statement: Define activation as the smallest event sequence that predicts D30 retention, then back-test on historical cohorts before locking the metric. Vanity activation rates that don't predict retention drive wrong optimizations.
rule.rationale: Winning variants on activation can cannibalise D30 retention if activation is defined as "completed onboarding tour" rather than "reached core value". The back-test is the safeguard.

list.item: SSO/social login — reduce step-one drop-off
Interactive product tours, not documentation pages
Progressive feature disclosure — no feature dump on first session
Contextual tooltips at moment of relevance, not pre-emptive
Personalized onboarding paths by user segment — 3-tour designs consistently beat 1-tour

rule.statement: Bake usage limits into the free tier from day one. Without a friction edge, there is no upgrade trigger and expansion revenue stalls.
rule.rationale: The "freemium tax" — free tier consuming infra cost but converting under 2% — is the direct consequence of a free tier with no usage gate. The upgrade trigger must exist in the product design, not in a sales email.

section.name: PLG Evolution (2026)
section.p: One-size-fits-all freemium is declining. The current pattern: tailored free tiers by user persona, AI-driven onboarding personalization, and hybrid PLG + sales-assist for enterprise segments above the SMB ceiling.

rule.statement: Pair PLG with sales-assist above the SMB ceiling — PLG expansion ceilings are real. The strategic question is when to add humans, not whether to add them.
rule.rationale: PQLs surface at the SMB ceiling and stall without a human follow-up motion. Expansion revenue stalls when no sales-assist exists to close team-wide or enterprise contracts.

rule.statement: Instrument the funnel before optimizing. Agents producing variants on uninstrumented funnels hill-climb noise — there is no signal to optimize against.
rule.rationale: PLG optimization is fundamentally an empirical loop; without telemetry, every "improvement" is untestable and every "result" is confirmation bias.

rule.statement: Keep one north-star metric (activation rate, NRR, or PQL-to-paid) per quarter. Rotate hypotheses, not metrics.
rule.rationale: Changing the north-star metric mid-quarter makes quarter-over-quarter comparison impossible and hides whether interventions are working or just moving a different number.

rule.statement: Gate every experiment behind SRM (sample-ratio mismatch) check and a pre-registered MDE (minimum detectable effect). Enforce minimum exposure of ≥1k users per variant and minimum runtime of ≥7 days before allowing a winner call.
rule.rationale: Both SRM and pre-registered MDE catch the most common "lift" mirages — exposing too few users or running too short produces statistically invalid conclusions that agents will confidently report as wins.

rule.statement: When optimizing onboarding, default to subtraction before addition. Prompt agents with "subtract first" and a step-count budget. Tour-builder agents tend to add steps; PLG wins usually come from removing friction.
rule.rationale: The cognitive load of each onboarding step adds drop-off risk. The empirical pattern across PLG products is that removing a step typically outperforms adding a better version of the same step.

rule.statement: Always require a retention guardrail metric alongside activation. Winning variants on activation that cannibalise D30 retention must be rejected.
rule.rationale: Activation and retention are not always aligned — a highly frictionless onboarding that skips important setup steps can produce activation wins that reverse by day 30.

rule.statement: Define PQL weights from in-product events that predict upgrade intent (workspace_created, second_user_invited, integration_added, limit_warning_seen), not vanity events (logged in, viewed dashboard). Score users as hot (≥12), warm (≥6), cold (below 6).
rule.rationale: PQL scoring based on vanity events produces a scoring system that identifies engaged free users, not users likely to pay. Upgrade-intent events (limit warnings, team invitations) predict conversion; pageviews do not.

rule.statement: Schedule quarterly PQL model recalibration. The event weights an agent ships today decay as the product changes; a static threshold becomes progressively wrong.
rule.rationale: Product changes alter which events predict upgrade intent. A PQL model trained on v1 behavior systematically mis-scores users on v3 behavior without recalibration.

antipattern.description: Running PLG optimization on a pre-PMF product with insufficient retention signal.
antipattern.reason: PLG amplifies a working loop; on a broken product it industrialises churn. Establish product-market fit before instrumenting the PLG funnel.

antipattern.description: AI onboarding personalization attempting to reference user behavior data before any events have been captured (cold start in first 60 seconds).
antipattern.reason: Enforce a "no behavioural reference before event N" rule. Cold-start personalization that references unavailable data produces error messages or hallucinated user attributes.

antipattern.description: Privacy regimes (GDPR, India DPDP) restricting event tracking that PLG analytics depend on, with consent-mode gaps distorting funnels.
antipattern.reason: Instrument consent-mode handling before running PLG analytics. Funnels measured without accounting for consent gaps systematically under-count users in regulated markets.

antipattern.description: "Freemium tax": free tier consumes infra cost but converts less than 2% — common when no usage-gated upgrade trigger exists.
antipattern.reason: Without a friction edge in the free tier, there is no upgrade trigger. The business model fails before the funnel optimization begins.

antipattern.description: TTV measured by clicks, not value: vanity activation rates that don't predict retention.
antipattern.reason: Optimizing on the wrong metric produces wrong interventions. Define activation as a retention-predictive event sequence, not a click count.

antipattern.description: One-size onboarding for distinct personas → low activation across all of them; segmentation is the unlock.
antipattern.reason: Personas have different value drivers. Single-tour designs lose to 3-tour designs almost every time.

antipattern.description: Sales-assist gap: PQLs surface but no human follow-up motion → expansion revenue stalls at SMB ceiling.
antipattern.reason: PQLs without sales support don't convert to team/enterprise deals. Add sales-assist at the SMB-to-enterprise inflection point.

rule.statement: Pricing and packaging changes require human approval. Revenue regression risk from autonomous pricing changes is non-recoverable — never delegate these to an agent without a human approval gate.
rule.rationale: An agent that can autonomously change a pricing tier can inadvertently destroy MRR for an entire customer segment in a single run. The failure mode is irreversible within the billing cycle.

rule.statement: Do not let agents touch consent or cookie banner logic. One mis-step breaks all downstream PLG telemetry.
rule.rationale: PLG analytics depend entirely on event capture; corrupting the consent layer silently breaks all measurement without an obvious error signal, causing strategy decisions to be made on invalid data.

rule.statement: Do not let agents autonomously change a stakeholder's engagement level or send communications to external users. These are relationship decisions with political and legal consequences.
rule.rationale: Automated communications to users without human review can violate communication preferences, trigger opt-outs, or send legally sensitive messages without approval.

antipattern.description: Agents over-fitting on small samples: calling a winner with fewer than 1k users per variant or fewer than 7 days of runtime.
antipattern.reason: Enforce the minimum exposure and runtime gates at the agent output layer, not just in the prompt. Agents will call winners on day 2 with 200 users per variant if not blocked.

antipattern.description: LLMs inventing metrics ("engagement score 0-100") that have no source event in the analytics schema.
antipattern.reason: Constrain output to a known event vocabulary loaded from the analytics schema. Invented metrics cannot be tracked or validated.

antipattern.description: Tour-builder agents adding steps rather than removing them, even when prompted to optimize onboarding.
antipattern.reason: Prompt explicitly with "subtract first" and a step-count budget. Monitor step-count delta as a guardrail metric alongside activation rate.

antipattern.description: PQL scoring drift: static model becomes wrong as the product evolves but continues to be used without recalibration.
antipattern.reason: Schedule quarterly PQL model recalibration as a standing SDD task. Flag if the model has not been recalibrated in 90+ days.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: PLG is an instrumentation-and-iteration loop, which maps cleanly to subagent orchestration. Use a researcher-style agent to inventory the current funnel (events, drop-offs, persona splits), a planner agent to propose an activation hypothesis with one metric to move, and an executor agent to ship the smallest in-product change (tour copy, default state, gate placement). Pair this with faion-sdd-execution so each PLG experiment ships through the same SDD quality gates as code, and use faion-brainstorm for diverging activation hypotheses before converging on the one to test. Keep humans in the loop for pricing/packaging changes — those are not safe to automate.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives an activation experiment as an SDD task: spec (hypothesis, metric, guardrail) → design (event schema, variant) → impl → test-plan (SRM check, MDE).
faion-brainstorm — diverge/converge on activation hypotheses and TTV interventions before committing to a test.
faion-improver — session loop to audit funnel telemetry, find leak with biggest delta, file improvement task.

section.name: CLI Tools and Services
section.p: Essential tools for PLG instrumentation and experimentation:

list.item: PostHog — Self-host product analytics, funnels, session replay, feature flags, A/B testing. https://posthog.com/docs/api
GrowthBook — OSS feature flagging + experimentation, Bayesian stats. https://docs.growthbook.io/tools/cli
Statsig — Feature gates, holdouts, exposure logs, PQL scoring. https://docs.statsig.com/sdks/cli
Amplitude API — Funnels, cohorts, export raw events for offline PQL scoring. https://www.docs.developers.amplitude.com
Mixpanel API — Funnels + JQL for custom PQL queries. https://developer.mixpanel.com
Segment / RudderStack — Event pipeline, ID resolution, downstream fanout. https://www.rudderstack.com/docs/cli
Stripe CLI — Subscription lifecycle, usage records, webhook listeners for upgrade events. https://docs.stripe.com/stripe-cli
dbt — Transform raw events → activation/PQL/NRR models in warehouse. https://docs.getdbt.com
Cube.dev — Semantic layer for PLG metrics (activation, TTV, NRR) shared across stack. https://cube.dev/docs
PostHog (OSS + Cloud) — Best default for agent-driven PLG; replays + flags + experiments in one.
GrowthBook (OSS + Cloud) — Cleanest experimentation API; pairs with any analytics warehouse.
Statsig (SaaS) — Strong holdout + sequential testing; PQL scoring built in.
Amplitude (SaaS) — Rich charts but agent ergonomics worse than PostHog.
Mixpanel (SaaS) — JQL good for ad-hoc PQL queries; less native experimentation.
Pendo / Appcues / Userflow (SaaS) — In-app tours; webhooks decent, code-as-source weak — risky for agent edits.
Frigade (SaaS) — Onboarding flows as code → agent can PR a tour.
Endgame / Pocus / Correlated (SaaS) — PQL signal layer; feeds reverse ETL into CRM for sales-assist.
Hightouch / Census (SaaS) — Reverse ETL warehouse → Salesforce/HubSpot for PQL handoff.
ProfitWell / ChartMogul (SaaS) — NRR, expansion, churn — the financial counterpart to activation metrics.
Stripe Billing (SaaS) — Usage-based billing, the engine behind expansion revenue.
Define activation as the smallest event sequence that predicts D30 retention; back-test before locking the metric.
Instrument before you optimise — agents producing variants on uninstrumented funnels will hill-climb noise.
Keep one north-star metric (activation rate, NRR, or PQL→paid) per quarter; rotate hypotheses, not metrics.
Gate experimentation behind SRM (sample-ratio mismatch) and pre-registered MDE; both catch most "lift" mirages.
Bake usage limits into the free tier from day one; without a friction edge there is no upgrade trigger.
Pair PLG with sales-assist above $X MRR — PLG ceilings are real; the question is when to add humans, not whether.
Persona-segment onboarding — single-tour designs lose to 3-tour designs almost every time, and AI personalisation works best layered on top of segments.
Treat copy and defaults as code: PR-reviewed, versioned, behind flags. Lets agents safely contribute.

section.name: Prompt Patterns for Agent Workflows
section.code: You are a PLG growth analyst. Given this funnel snapshot (<events JSON>) and persona segment (<segment>), identify the single largest drop-off where lift > MDE 3pp is plausible in 14d. Output: hypothesis, metric, guardrail metric, smallest-change variant. No copy that requires legal review. Return strict JSON.

You are an onboarding editor. Rewrite step tooltip so that TTV for persona

drops below 5 minutes. Constraints: <=14 words, no marketing language, must reference the action the user just took. Output 3 variants + rationale per variant.

template.purpose: PostHog-based PQL scorer: weight in-app events, write pql_score + pql_tier back as person properties

see-also.link: ProductLed Institute — frameworks and case studies
Reforge — Growth Loops and PLG curricula
